teaching_arc.entry_point: Students know functions and trig from Session 1, but may still think a limit equals the function value at a point.
teaching_arc.progression: Open with approach intuition on a parabola, formalize the limit definition with a hole, build one-sided limits and existence, run epsilon–delta lab, table pause, misconception trap, jump discontinuity pause, optional oscillation challenge, sum law bridge, recap.
teaching_arc.destination: Students can read limits from graphs and tables, test one-sided agreement, separate f(a) from the limit, and name why a limit fails.
teaching_arc.tone: Clear, warm, visual, active, and personal-brand friendly.
methods: Use interactive limit graphs as the main explanation surface, not static plot placeholders.
Use short worked steps for examples so students can follow without a wall of text.
Name the f(a) vs limit mistake before it becomes a habit.
During narrated playback, move probe x, ε, or zoom when the instructor names them.

audience_level: first-year university and advanced high-school students who completed Topics 1–2 (functions and trig) and are starting Session 2 limits
topic_goal: Build intuitive and graphical fluency with limits, approach, one-sided limits, existence, and the epsilon–delta picture, before algebraic limit laws.
topic_takeaways: A limit describes nearby approach, not necessarily f(a).
One-sided limits must agree for a two-sided limit to exist.
Removable holes, jumps, and oscillations show three ways limits behave at trouble points.
Tables and graphs both reveal approach values.
Sum law previews the algebraic toolkit in Topic 4, Limit Laws.
style_notes: Keep the tone personal, warm, and precise, as if teaching directly to a YouTube viewer.
Welcome students back to Arian University, do NOT re-introduce Arian from scratch; Topics 1–2 already did that.
On learning-objective slides, orient students on the course roadmap before listing skills.
Name Topics 1–2 as completed, this topic as current, Topic 4 Limit Laws as next.
Move between graph, formula, table, and plain-language interpretation on every example.
Use student-prediction moments before revealing limits, existence, or misconception answers.
When a widget is present, call out probe x, ε, or zoom, then let the scripted timeline move while explaining.
For website mode, invite students to drag controls after the narrated pass.
On dense visual slides, keep the avatar low so the graph carries focus.
For pause slides, give a real beat, then answer with one concrete number or one-sided pair.
transition_style: Use each slide as one teaching beat: name the task, point to the visual, move the interactive state, preview the next representation.

slide_ref.source_title: The Intuitive Concept of a Limit

slides.narration_seed: Welcome back to Session 2, Limits. The word approach is the whole game. Quick hook: as x nears 2 on x², outputs march to 4 even if we never 'plug in' 2.
must_cover: Welcome back, Topic 3, first topic of Session 2 Limits and Continuity.
Session 1 (functions + trig) is the foundation; limits ask what happens near a point.
This session stays visual and interactive, graphs and tables before heavy algebra.
Topic 4 Limit Laws comes next for computing limits faster.
must_say: Calculus starts with one idea: approach.
A limit tells us where a function is heading, not necessarily where it lands.
slides.transition_from_previous: Topic 2 gave periodic intuition; Topic 3 opens the limit chapter.
slides.transition_to_next: Show the roadmap and four skills for this session.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think limits are just substitution. Tease that holes break substitution immediately.
slides.explanation_style: Brief welcome-back, then immediate approach hook, no long re-introduction.
slides.story_hint: Walking toward a wall, distance goes to zero even if you stop short.
slides.delivery_goal: Students feel Session 2 has begun within 15 seconds.
slides.scene_hint: Face camera for welcome-back, then gesture toward the approaching-x widget.

slides.narration_seed: Roadmap: Topics 1–2 done, Topic 3 current, Topic 4 next. Four objectives, graphs/tables, one-sided limits, f(a) vs limit, epsilon–delta + sum law preview.
must_cover: Point to the course roadmap before reading objectives.
Session 2 framing: Limits and Continuity; five topics in this session.
Do not say topic X of Y aloud, the grid shows progress.
Four skills map to the four blocks of the lecture.
slides.transition_from_previous: Title named the session; this slide turns it into a promise.
slides.transition_to_next: Drop into the approach hook on the parabola.
likely_student_confusion: Too many representations at once, tell them each block adds one tool.
slides.explanation_style: Brisk checklist; do not read every roadmap cell.
slides.story_hint: Session 1 built language; Session 2 asks about nearby behavior.
slides.delivery_goal: Students know where they are in the course and what today covers.

slides.narration_seed: Approach metaphor + parabola widget. Animate x: 0 → 1.5 → 1.9 → 1.99; f(x) → 4, |x−2| shrinks.
must_cover: Limit = destination the function approaches.
Arrival at the point is not required.
Today: graphs, tables, one-sided limits, epsilon–delta picture.
must_say: The word approach is all you need to start.
slides.transition_from_previous: Objectives listed skills; now show the core picture.
slides.transition_to_next: Formalize with notation and a hole example.
likely_student_confusion: Confusing x approaching a with actually reaching a.
slides.explanation_style: Let the moving point narrate; keep prose short.
slides.delivery_goal: Students can say 'approach' in their own words with the parabola example.

slides.narration_seed: Read theorem_box once. Rational hole (x²−4)/(x−2): undefined at 2, limit 4. Drag x from both sides toward the open circle.
must_cover: Symbolic limit notation lim x→a f(x) = L.
f(a) need not exist or equal L.
Removable hole at (2, 4) as the canonical example.
must_say: What matters is nearby behavior, not the point itself.
slides.transition_from_previous: Intuition on parabola; now notation + hole.
slides.transition_to_next: One-sided limits when left and right stories differ.
likely_student_confusion: Thinking undefined at x=a means no limit.
slides.explanation_style: Theorem first, then one graph proof.
slides.delivery_goal: Students connect notation to the open-circle picture.

slides.narration_seed: Define x→a⁻ and x→a⁺. |x|/x: left −1, right +1. Timeline sweeps x negative to positive.
must_cover: Left-hand limit: smaller x values.
Right-hand limit: larger x values.
|x|/x plateaus differ, two-sided limit will fail.
slides.transition_from_previous: Two-sided definition assumed one story; now split the story.
slides.transition_to_next: State the existence test: sides must match.
likely_student_confusion: Mixing up which side is negative vs positive for |x|/x.
slides.explanation_style: Name sides, then drive x across 0.
slides.story_hint: Two doors into the same point, different hallways.
slides.delivery_goal: Students read one-sided notation and match it to graph branches.

slide_ref.source_title: When Does a Two-Sided Limit Exist?

slides.narration_seed: IFF statement. Piecewise at 2: branches → 3, f(2)=4. Drag x along each branch; hollow vs solid dot.
must_cover: Two-sided limit exists iff one-sided limits exist and are equal.
f(2)=4 while limit=3, both true.
Preview misconception slide explicitly.
must_say: Equal one-sided limits, that is the whole test.
slides.transition_from_previous: One-sided limits defined; now combine them.
likely_student_confusion: Assuming the filled dot is the limit.
slides.explanation_style: State rule, then show dot vs hollow.
slides.story_hint: Branches agree at 3; party at 4 is separate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students can apply the existence test on a graph.

slides.narration_seed: Full-width visual_lab. f(x)=x², a=2, L=4. Shrink ε, move probe x; green point when inside ε-band. Intuition only, not a full proof drill.
must_cover: ε-band on outputs around L.
δ-band on inputs around a (computed in widget).
Smaller ε usually forces smaller usable δ.
Formal definition named once; depth comes later.
slides.transition_from_previous: Existence test was graphical; now precision bands.
slides.explanation_style: Shorter narration; let bands and colors teach.
slides.story_hint: Tolerance zone on outputs demands a closeness zone on inputs.
slides.delivery_goal: Students connect epsilon–delta to the approach idea, not fear it.

slide_ref.source_title: Pause: Estimate a Limit from a Table

slides.narration_seed: YouTube pause. Table for (x²−1)/(x−1). Read left column then right. Widget mirrors x→1. Silence for guess.
must_cover: Table shows approach from both sides.
f(1) undefined, ignore the point.
Pattern → limit 2.
must_say: Pause here and write your guess before continuing.
slides.transition_from_previous: Lab was formal; table is data-driven.
slides.explanation_style: Real pause beat, then reveal block.
slides.delivery_goal: Students trust tables for approach values.

slides.narration_seed: Answer: limit 2. Simplify to x+2 for x≠1. Widget confirms hole at (1,2). Mention derivative preview.
must_cover: Limit = 2.
Removable discontinuity / hole.
0/0 pattern returns in derivatives.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause guess; now close the loop.
slides.transition_to_next: Name the f(a) misconception before more examples.
slides.explanation_style: Short steps; one line on derivatives foreshadow.
slides.delivery_goal: Students see table, graph, and algebra agree.

slides.narration_seed: misconception_compare pairs. Same piecewise: f(2)=4, limit 3. emphasizeMisconception on widget.
must_cover: Wrong: limit equals f(a) because of the dot.
Right: limit reads branch approach, hollow at 3.
Repeat: both statements can be true simultaneously.
must_say: The limit is not the dot, it is the nearby trend.
slides.transition_from_previous: Table example worked; now the biggest conceptual trap.
slides.transition_to_next: Second pause, does |x|/x have a two-sided limit?
likely_student_confusion: Students double down on the green dot.
slides.explanation_style: Read pairs clearly; point at hollow vs solid.
slides.delivery_goal: Students stop equating f(a) with the limit.

slides.narration_seed: YouTube pause on |x|/x. Widget hides side labels, question mark only. Demand Yes/No plus one-sided values.
must_cover: Left approaches −1.
Right approaches +1.
Unequal → two-sided limit DNE.
must_say: Pause, does the two-sided limit exist?
slides.transition_from_previous: Misconception was about dots; this is about disagreeing sides.
likely_student_confusion: Saying DNE because f(0) is undefined, clarify one-sided limits still exist.
slides.explanation_style: Silence for thinking; require one-sided numbers.
slides.story_hint: Callback to slide 4 with a test.
slides.delivery_goal: Students apply existence test without hints.

slides.narration_seed: Reveal: left −1, right +1, DNE. Jump discontinuity vocabulary. Widget shows labeled branches.
must_cover: One-sided limits exist but differ.
Two-sided limit does not exist.
Jump discontinuity, clean failure mode.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause answer; name the discontinuity type.

slides.narration_seed: Optional, skip if time-limited. Zoom sin(1/x); frequency explodes. Limit DNE by oscillation. Sets up future Squeeze topic.
must_cover: Oscillations densify near 0.
Different sequences → different approach values.
Third failure mode beyond jumps and holes.
slides.transition_from_previous: Jump was a clean split; oscillation is messy.
likely_student_confusion: Thinking graph 'looks continuous' enough for a limit.
slides.explanation_style: Let zoom slider narrate; keep theory light.
slides.delivery_goal: Students know limits can fail without a visible jump.

slides.narration_seed: theorem_box sum law. Widget: f→3, g→5, sum→8 at x=1. Bridge to Topic 4 Limit Laws, full toolkit next.
must_cover: Limit of sum = sum of limits when both exist.
Graphical confirmation at x=1.
Topic 4 adds product, quotient, and computation shortcuts.
slides.transition_from_previous: Intuition complete; one algebraic law preview.
likely_student_confusion: Applying sum law when one limit DNE.
slides.explanation_style: State law, show green sum curve.
slides.delivery_goal: Students see why limit laws will matter next video.

slides.narration_seed: Six bullets, map each to a moment in the video. Teaser Topic 4 Limit Laws. End warmly; Session 2 is underway.
must_cover: Approach vs arrival; f(a) optional.
One-sided agreement test.
Hole, jump, oscillation failure modes.
Tables and epsilon–delta intuition.
Sum law preview.
Next: Limit Laws and Algebraic Evaluation.
slides.transition_from_previous: Sum law previewed algebra; summary is the checklist.
slides.transition_to_next: Topic 4, Limit Laws and Algebraic Evaluation.
likely_student_confusion: Leaving without the f(a) vs limit separation, restate once.
slides.explanation_style: One takeaway per breath; point backward to widgets.
slides.story_hint: Close Session 2 opener; algebra speed comes next.
slides.delivery_goal: Students leave with a limit toolkit and know what Topic 4 assumes.
